avoid: webpage (when referring to the full site)
project (when referring specifically to the published web property)
app (unless the output is actually an app)
product (when referring specifically to a website)
terms.definition: A web property created and managed by a user using Sticklight. A site or website is the user-facing outcome of a Sticklight project. Users create a project in Sticklight, and the result can be a website they edit, preview, publish, and manage.

when_to_use: Use site in product UI, navigation, settings, and instructional copy
Use website when clarity or natural phrasing is more important than brevity
When referring to the published or publishable web experience created from a project
When explaining what users are building with Sticklight
when_not_to_use: When referring to the saved item in Sticklight's project library — use project
When referring to one page inside a website — use page
When referring to apps, dashboards, stores, or other non-website outputs
When referring to the Workspace or creation surface
notes: site is preferred for brevity and consistency
website is acceptable when it sounds more natural or improves clarity
Use project for the saved workspace/item in Sticklight
Use site or website for the web output users create and publish
Avoid using website when the project is actually an app, dashboard, store, or other digital product

correct: Describe the website you want to create.
Run a security scan before publishing your site.
Your site is ready to publish.
avoid: Open your website from My projects.

terms.definition: Reusable workflow instructions that tell Sticklight how to handle a specific build task. Skills are written as markdown files and define the structure, logic, standards, and expected output Sticklight should follow when working on a project.

when_to_use: When referring to reusable task-specific instructions uploaded to Sticklight
When describing how users can standardize repeated workflows
When explaining how Sticklight follows a user's preferred logic, structure, or standards
In library, upload, invocation, and project workflows
when_not_to_use: When referring to a visual starting point for a project — use template
When referring to a one-off prompt or request
When referring broadly to all saved resources or files
notes: Always capitalize as Skills when referring to the Sticklight system feature
A skill is not a template: templates provide a starting structure; Skills define how Sticklight should work
Skills are created as SKILL.md files
Each skill should focus on one clear, repeatable task
Use plain, practical language; avoid making Skills sound overly technical or developer-only
Preferred framing: 'Build it once, reuse it across projects'

examples.correct: Browse the Sticklight library for ready-made Skills built by our team. Frontend design, performance, accessibility, and more.
examples.avoid: Upload a template to tell Sticklight how to handle this workflow.

terms.definition: A curated library of ready-made Skills built by the Sticklight team.

when_to_use: When referring to the built-in collection of Sticklight-made Skills
When describing ready-made Skills users can browse, apply, or reuse
In flows related to discovering, selecting, or adding Skills to a project
when_not_to_use: When referring to custom Skills uploaded by the user
When referring to project templates or visual starting points
When referring broadly to all saved resources in Sticklight
notes: Always capitalize as Skills Library
Refers only to ready-made Skills created by Sticklight
Avoid shortening to Library unless context is completely clear
Position as a way to discover proven workflows, not as a generic asset library

examples.correct: Browse the Skills Library to find ready-made workflows for accessibility, performance, and frontend design.
examples.avoid: Browse the Templates Library to find instructions for how Sticklight should work.

terms.definition: The main starting point in Sticklight where users begin a new project by describing what they want to create.

when_to_use: When referring to the first screen users see before creating a project
When describing where users enter a prompt to start building
In navigation, empty states, onboarding, and instructional copy
In flows that return users from a project back to the starting area
when_not_to_use: When referring to an active project after creation
When referring to the user's list of existing projects
When referring to account, billing, or settings areas
When referring generically to the Sticklight product
notes: Always capitalize as Workspace when referring to the Sticklight product area
The Workspace is where creation starts, not where project editing happens
Avoid using dashboard unless the screen is focused on managing existing projects or account-level activity

correct: Back to Workspace
Start a new project from the Workspace.
avoid: Enter your prompt on the dashboard.
Read-only mode (unless technical clarification is needed)
terms.definition: A mode where Sticklight analyzes, explores, and plans the next steps before making changes. Sticklight can understand the request, ask clarifying questions, review context, and propose an approach without creating or changing anything until the user approves.

when_to_use: When referring to the mode that helps users plan before building
In tooltips, mode switchers, onboarding, and instructional copy
When explaining that Sticklight will not make changes yet
when_not_to_use: When Sticklight is actively creating or changing a project
When referring to a saved plan or build summary
notes: Use Plan mode for the full mode name
Use Plan only as the short button label when space is limited
Written in sentence case in UI
Pair naturally with build/create language: plan first, build after approval

correct: Plan your next steps before building.
Close Plan mode.
avoid: Enter Research mode to explore your options.

terms.definition: A view that hides the site or product being built, so users can interact with Sticklight as a pure conversational agent.

when_to_use: When referring to the view that hides the build preview
In tooltips, view controls, onboarding, and instructional copy
when_not_to_use: When referring to Plan mode
When referring to minimizing or closing the AI panel
When referring to browser fullscreen behavior
notes: Use Focus mode for the full mode name
Written in sentence case in UI
Focus mode changes the view, not Sticklight's behavior
Keep the distinction clear: Plan mode controls whether Sticklight plans or builds; Focus mode controls what the user sees

correct: Enter Focus mode.
Use Focus mode to chat with Sticklight without the build preview.
avoid: Hide the website to focus on AI.

terms.definition: A mode where users can directly edit and refine the project Sticklight created.

when_to_use: When referring to the mode for manually editing a generated project
In tooltips, mode switchers, onboarding, and instructional copy
when_not_to_use: When Sticklight is generating or changing the project through chat
When referring to Plan mode
When referring to publishing or deployment
notes: Use Edit mode for the full mode name
Written in sentence case in UI
'Refine' can be used as a verb, but not as the mode name
Keep the distinction clear: Plan mode is for deciding what to do; Edit mode is for changing the project directly

correct: Enter Edit mode.
Use Edit mode to refine the layout and customize the design.
avoid: Enter Refine mode to customize your site.
Use Manual mode for pixel-perfect control.
Left panel (unless describing placement in support/debugging context)
terms.definition: The panel where users chat with Sticklight to create, build, refine, and troubleshoot a project.

when_to_use: When referring to the left-side panel where the user interacts with Sticklight
In tooltips, view controls, onboarding, and instructional copy
when_not_to_use: When referring to the project preview or editable canvas
When referring to Focus mode
When referring to the prompt input only
notes: Use Chat panel for the full UI area
Written in sentence case in UI
The Chat panel can be shown, hidden, or toggled
Avoid overusing 'AI' in UI labels when Chat panel is clearer

correct: Toggle Chat panel.
Open the Chat panel to continue working with Sticklight.
avoid: Open the AI panel to build your site.
Use the left panel to ask Sticklight for changes.

approved: Build (when referring to the actual construction process or output)
avoid: Start (when the action creates a project)
terms.definition: The primary action for turning a user's prompt into a new Sticklight project.

when_to_use: As the main CTA for starting project creation
In onboarding, prompt input, empty states, and primary action flows
when_not_to_use: When referring to planning before execution — use Plan
When referring to manual refinement — use Edit
When referring to publishing a finished project
notes: Use Create as the main CTA
Avoid 'generate' when it makes Sticklight feel like a one-shot AI output
Pair naturally with Plan: plan before building, create when ready

correct: Create
Describe the website you want to create.
Create and publish websites, apps, dashboards, and stores.
avoid: Make a website from your prompt.
Library (when referring to the full Tools panel)
terms.definition: A set of pre-built resources users can add to a Sticklight project, including widgets, packages, and integrations.

when_to_use: When referring to the full Tools panel
When describing the entry point for widgets, packages, and integrations
when_not_to_use: When referring only to reusable workflows — use Skills
When referring to visual components only — use widgets
When referring to JavaScript libraries only — use packages
When referring to connected third-party services only — use integrations
notes: Always capitalize Tools when referring to the Sticklight product area
The Tools panel includes three categories: Widgets, Packages, and Integrations

examples.correct: Use Tools to add widgets, packages, and integrations to your project.
examples.avoid: Use assets to enhance your website.

avoid: Sections (unless referring to a specific layout section)
terms.definition: Pre-built UI components users can add to a Sticklight project.

when_to_use: When referring to pre-built visual components in the Tools panel
In Tools panel tabs, cards, filters, and add flows
when_not_to_use: When referring to JavaScript libraries — use packages
When referring to API-based connections — use integrations
When referring to full project starting points — use templates
When referring to custom instructions — use Skills
notes: Use Widgets as the tab name
Use lowercase widget in running text
Keep the distinction clear: widgets are pre-built items users add; elements are parts of the project users select and edit

correct: Add a widget to your project.
Select one or more widgets to add them to your site.
examples.avoid: Add a package to create a contact form.

terms.definition: JavaScript libraries users can add to a Sticklight project for advanced functionality.

when_to_use: When referring to JavaScript libraries available in the Tools panel
In Tools panel tabs, package cards, installation flows, and technical guidance
when_not_to_use: When referring to visual UI components — use widgets
When referring to third-party services that require account connection or API keys — use integrations
When referring to Sticklight workflow instructions — use Skills
notes: Use Packages as the tab name
Use lowercase package in running text
Avoid encouraging users to add unnecessary packages; each package may affect performance

correct: Add a package to use advanced animations in your project.
Use GSAP to animate the hero section on scroll.
examples.avoid: Add an integration to create scroll animations.

avoid: Connections (unless referring to the connection state)
terms.definition: Third-party services users can connect to a Sticklight project.

when_to_use: When referring to third-party services available in the Tools panel
In setup flows that require API keys, credentials, or service configuration
when_not_to_use: When referring to UI components — use widgets
When referring to JavaScript libraries — use packages
When referring to reusable workflows — use Skills
notes: Use Integrations as the tab name
Use lowercase integration in running text
Be clear when an integration requires credentials or an API key
Integrations connect external services; they do not create visual UI by themselves

correct: Add an integration to connect Google Analytics.
Enter your API key to connect this integration.
examples.avoid: Install a plugin to connect analytics.

avoid: Sites (when referring to the saved item in Sticklight)
Websites (when referring to the full saved item)
terms.definition: The saved digital products users create and manage in Sticklight. A project can be a website, app, dashboard, store, or another digital experience.

when_to_use: When referring to the saved items users create in Sticklight
In project cards, filters, search, status labels, and management flows
when_not_to_use: When referring specifically to a published website — use published site or live site
When referring to reusable workflows — use Skills
When referring to pre-built components — use Widgets
notes: Use My projects as the library name
Use lowercase project in running text
Status labels: Published / Not published
Avoid narrowing the term to 'site' unless the specific project type is clearly a site

correct: Open a project from My projects.
View all published and not published projects.
avoid: Open a website from My projects.

terms.definition: A status that shows Sticklight is interpreting the user's request, reviewing context, and deciding what to do next. When complete, the status changes to Thought.

when_to_use: When Sticklight is evaluating a request before taking action
In chat activity states
when_not_to_use: When Sticklight is running a specific tool or action — use Running
When Sticklight is directly changing project files — use Editing
notes: Use Thinking for the active state
Use Thought for the completed state

correct: Thinking
Thought

terms.definition: A status that shows Sticklight is using a tool or performing a specific system action. When complete, the status changes to Ran.

when_to_use: When Sticklight performs a tool-based action
In chat activity states where the action name appears next to the status
when_not_to_use: When Sticklight is only interpreting the request — use Thinking
When Sticklight is directly changing a file or project — use Editing
notes: Use Running for the active state
Use Ran for the completed state
Pair with a specific action name when available

correct: Running Codebase map
Ran Codebase map

terms.definition: A status that shows Sticklight is making changes to a project file or part of the build. When complete, the status changes to Edited.

when_to_use: When Sticklight is directly changing the project
In chat activity states where Sticklight is applying requested changes
when_not_to_use: When the user is manually changing the project in Edit mode
When Sticklight is only planning or interpreting the request — use Thinking
When Sticklight is running a tool without changing the project — use Running
notes: Use Editing for the active state
Use Edited for the completed state
This is a system activity state, not the same as Edit mode

correct: Editing ContactForm.tsx
Edited ContactForm.tsx

approved: brand kit (for the visual identity set)
also_allowed: brand assets (when referring to uploaded or provided brand materials)
color palette (when referring specifically to colors)
avoid: Design system (when referring to the selectable UI feature)
Palette (when referring to the full kit)
Look and feel (as a UI label)
terms.definition: The Sticklight area for defining a project's visual identity. Users open Brand to choose or customize a brand kit — the set of colors, fonts, and styling choices that guide how Sticklight designs the project.

when_to_use: Use Brand as the short UI label
Use brand kit when referring to the selected or customized visual identity set
when_not_to_use: When referring only to colors — use colors or color palette
When referring only to typography — use fonts
notes: Use Brand for the compact UI label
Use lowercase brand kit in running text
Brand is the entry point; brand kit is the visual identity set

correct: Brand
Select a brand kit.
Customize your brand kit to use your own colors and fonts.
avoid: Open Style to choose your project's look and feel.

approved: version (for a single saved project state)
avoid: Versions (when referring to the full view)
terms.definition: A view where users can see and restore saved versions of a Sticklight project.

when_to_use: When referring to the full view that lists saved project versions
In navigation, tooltips, project controls, and restore flows
when_not_to_use: When referring to chat messages or conversation history
When referring to publishing history
notes: Written in sentence case in UI
Avoid developer-only terms like commit in user-facing copy
Make restore copy clear about impact: restoring a version may replace the current project state

correct: Version history
View and restore saved versions.
Restore this version.

avoid: Agent (unless officially supported as a product term)
terms.definition: The technology in Sticklight that interprets user input, follows instructions, and helps create or refine projects. Use AI when referring to the model's ability to understand context, follow guidance, or interpret uploaded materials. Use Sticklight when referring to the product, platform, interface, or overall experience.

when_to_use: When explaining how prompts, uploads, Skills, or brand inputs improve the system's output
When the subject is the intelligence behind the output, not the product surface
when_not_to_use: When referring to the product or platform as a whole — use Sticklight
When referring to UI areas, panels, settings, or controls
notes: Always uppercase AI
Do not spell out artificial intelligence
Preferred framing: users guide the AI; Sticklight provides the environment where the work happens

correct: Guide the AI with clear instructions.
Uploads help the AI understand your vision more precisely.
Use Skills to teach the AI how to handle repeatable tasks.
avoid: Use artificial intelligence to create your site.
Widget (unless referring to a pre-built widget from Tools)
terms.definition: An individual part of a project that users can select and edit in the preview.

when_to_use: When referring to something users can click, select, or edit in the preview
In Edit mode, visual editing, and properties panel copy
when_not_to_use: When referring to pre-built items users add from Tools — use widget
When referring to reusable workflow instructions — use Skill
notes: Use element for selectable, editable parts of the project
Keep the distinction clear: widgets are pre-built items users add; elements are parts of the project users select and edit

correct: Select an element.
Click any element in the preview to start editing.
avoid: Click any block to edit its style.

terms.definition: The view where users can see and interact with the current project output.

when_to_use: When referring to the default visual view of the project
In top navigation, view controls, onboarding, and instructional copy
when_not_to_use: When referring to the published version of the project
When referring to Edit mode
When referring to the live public URL
notes: Use Preview as the top navigation label
Avoid implying the project is live or public

correct: Preview
Open Preview to see your project.

terms.definition: The top navigation area where users directly edit the visual parts of a project.

when_to_use: As the top navigation label for visual editing
When distinguishing direct visual changes from chat-based changes
when_not_to_use: When referring to planning before building — use Plan mode
When referring to code-level changes — use Code
When referring to Sticklight's system activity state — use Editing / Edited
notes: Use Edit as the top navigation label
Keep the distinction clear: Edit is the view/control; Editing is an AI activity state

correct: Edit
Open Edit to adjust spacing, colors, and typography.
examples.avoid: Open Design to customize the page.

avoid: Code editor (as the top navigation label)
terms.definition: The view where users can inspect or work with the project's underlying code.

when_to_use: As the top navigation label for the code view
When users can inspect, review, or edit project code
when_not_to_use: When referring to visual editing — use Edit
When referring to the generated project as a whole — use project
notes: Use Code as the top navigation label
Keep the distinction clear: Edit is visual control; Code is implementation-level control

correct: Code
Open Code to review the project files.

avoid: Tables (when referring to the full view)
terms.definition: The view where users manage project data and database tables.

when_to_use: As the top navigation label for the project's data area
In empty states, setup flows, and data management copy
when_not_to_use: When referring to general content on a page
When referring to third-party services — use Integrations
notes: Use Database as the top navigation label
Avoid abbreviating to DB in user-facing copy

correct: Database
Tables will appear here once you create them in your database.
Ask AI to set data.
examples.avoid: Open Backend to manage your data.

avoid: Scanner (when referring to the full view)
Audit (unless specifically referring to a scan/audit result)
terms.definition: The view where users check and manage security-related controls for a project.

when_to_use: As the top navigation label for the security area
When referring to scans, controls, failures, warnings, and passed checks
when_not_to_use: When referring to general product safety or privacy
When referring to publishing status
notes: Use Security as the top navigation label
Keep severity labels clear: Critical, Warnings, Info, Passed

correct: Security
Run a security scan before publishing.
Security Controls
examples.avoid: Open Protection to scan your project.

avoid: Project settings (as the top navigation label)
terms.definition: The view where users manage project-level settings and operational details.

when_to_use: As the top navigation label for project settings
When users need to update project information, domains, access, or operational settings
when_not_to_use: When referring to visual styling — use Brand or brand kit
When referring to direct visual editing — use Edit
notes: Use Settings as the top navigation label
Use more specific labels inside the view: Project info, Project instructions, Domains, and Logs

correct: Settings
Open Settings to edit project info.
Add guidelines to shape how the AI builds your project.

terms.definition: Visual starting points users can apply as the foundation for a new Sticklight project. Templates are production-ready websites from the Lovable community. They help users begin with an existing structure, layout, and visual direction instead of starting from a blank prompt.

when_to_use: When referring to pre-built websites users can start from
When users browse, select, or apply a visual starting point
In project creation, onboarding, and template selection flows
When explaining that a selected template becomes the project foundation
when_not_to_use: When referring to reusable workflow instructions — use Skills
When referring to pre-built components users add to a project — use Widgets
When referring to JavaScript libraries — use Packages
When referring to brand styling options — use Brand or brand kit
notes: Use Templates for the category or collection
Use lowercase template in running text unless it starts a sentence or appears as a UI label
Templates are not Skills: a template defines what the project starts from; a Skill defines how the AI should work
Templates are not Widgets: a template is a full project foundation; a widget is a component added to a project
Avoid implying templates are final or fixed; users can customize them through chat, Edit mode, and Code

correct: Start your next project with a template.
Begin with a template.
The selected template is applied as the project foundation.
avoid: Start your next project with a Skill.
Choose a theme to build your project.
Customize (when referring to the initial template-based creation flow)
terms.definition: An action that lets users start from a template and make it their own. Remix applies a template as the project foundation and lets users add guidance before generation, so the AI can adapt the template with the right context from the start.

when_to_use: When users start a new project from a template
When users can add guidance before the template becomes their project
When the goal is to adapt an existing template, not copy it exactly
In template galleries, template detail pages, and project creation flows
when_not_to_use: When creating an exact copy of an existing project — use Duplicate
When referring to saving or reusing a project without changes
When referring to editing a project after it already exists
When referring to adding a widget, package, or integration
notes: Use Remix when the user starts from a template and adapts it
Remix is not a 1:1 copy; it includes user guidance and AI adaptation
Remix should feel creative and flexible, not technical
Keep the distinction clear: Remix adapts a template; Duplicate copies a project
Use template as the object: users remix templates, not Skills or Widgets

correct: Remix this template.
Add guidance to remix this template for your project.
Remix a template, then refine it through chat or Edit mode.
avoid: Duplicate this template to make it your own.

terms.definition: An action that creates a 1:1 version of an existing project. Duplicate creates a separate copy of the original project that users can later edit, publish, or manage as their own project.

when_to_use: When users create an exact copy of an existing project
When the new project should preserve the original structure, content, and setup
In project menus, project management flows, and reuse actions
When users want a safe copy they can edit without changing the original
when_not_to_use: When starting from a template with added guidance — use Remix
When applying a template as a new project foundation
When making small edits to the current project
When saving a version in Version history
notes: Use Duplicate for exact project copies
A duplicated project becomes a separate project
Duplicating does not adapt the project with AI guidance by default
Avoid developer-oriented terms like fork unless the product formally supports that model
Keep the distinction clear: Duplicate copies an existing project; Remix adapts a template with new guidance

correct: Duplicate project.
Create a duplicate to edit this project without changing the original.
avoid: Remix this project to create an exact copy.
